10X Advisor Network: Welcome to our 10X bonus session on strategic
transformations and financing.
We’re joined by Dwayne Baker, Cardone Venture’s Executive Vice President of
Strategic Transformation and Partnerships. He runs point on all Cardone Venture’s
M&A activity.
Also, we have Jeff Jessen with us. Jeff’s Cardone Venture’s Executive Vice President of
Finance.
We knew we had to talk more with you both after hearing Oliver Fernandez’s story
(see his acquisition case study module in the bonus section). He mentioned that the
both of you were invaluable in his own acquisition journey.
Thank you for being with us!
Jeff & Dwayne: Thanks for having us.
10X Advisor Network: Before we dive in, can you give us your backgrounds and
what you’re responsible for within Cardone Ventures?
Dwayne Baker: Sure, absolutely, I'll go first. I’m executive vice president of strategic
transformation and partnership here at Cardone Ventures. My background, I come
from 14 years at a large global consulting, big four accounting consulting firm, Ernst
& Young.
My role there, I was in our transformation practice. I had a smattering of roles over
the years, but I'm an accountant by trade. I've had a number of roles doing
transformation, involving technology for large global fortune 50 brands. I assisted in
transactions that they would do. Say, for example, they acquired a company. We would
go in and look at some of the processes they have in place, how they would integrate
with existing processes for the acquirer. And then identify any blind spots that would
be there.
We would also make sure that we could integrate and operate and help them identify if
they're paying too much or any other risk around the deal.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 15 --

Jeff Jessen: And I'm the executive vice president of finance here at Cardone Ventures.
And my background also started in public accounting. And then eventually, I moved
my way into advisory services and ultimately ended up in a family office which had
about $750 million in assets under management. Those were operating companies we
had acquired a majority stake in.
We either held those assets and grew them and created returns off of those… or, we
would sell those assets.
During a 15-year tenure there, I held various positions as managing director of the
portfolio as a whole, as well as CFO of several of the assets that we had acquired. I was
also heavily involved in the merger and acquisition activity related to those assets.
10X Advisor Network: What’s the number one mistake you’ve seen a buyer make in
the acquisition process? Both you Jeff, on the finance side and you Dwayne on the
operations side?
Dwayne Baker: First, when any process to buy a deal evolves around valuation or
what you're going to pay for the company. Two, the due diligence around that. And
then three, the actual merger agreement or purchase agreement for the deal.
When you think about those steps, what am I going to pay for the company and how
are you going to value that?
I'll let Jeff talk about that some more with something called add backs.
But from a due diligence and operation perspective, the number one mistake is just
not knowing what you're buying.
But you want this business for a reason.
That mom and pop could have a very large customer concentration. That mom and
pop may go to church with these people. It's a relationship they built over 50 years.
And as soon as mom and pop go away, these individuals may not be your customers
anymore.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 15 --

If you look at a business’ customer list and the transactions and things that are being
done, as much as 80% of someone's business could be one, two, five parties.
And if one of those go away based on the relationship they had with the previous
owner, you're going to put yourself in a big hole from the beginning.
Especially, if you think about how you're going to pay for the business. Or, if you're
trying to cover debt payments or cover payroll. If you lose a large customer early on, it
could impact you negatively.
Secondly, is any specific supplier agreements that you may have. Make sure any
contracts the business has are transferable to the new owner and that you're not out
there negotiating new pricing because most of the valuation and what you're paying
for the business is going to be determined on what they're currently making in
revenue, what the cost of the current materials are, so you can get certain gross
margins and net margins on that.
If you can’t preserve those supplier contracts, you may not be able to continue to
operate that business in the same manner that the previous owner was. So be aware of
that.
And always when you think about valuation. There’s only one thing you can't change
in a business, and that's the price you paid. So you have to make sure that you're
dialed in on what you're paying for it, and that you can actually not only recoup the
premium that you're paying for it, but that you have a plan in place to add some value
and increase cash flow in the business.
Jeff Jessen: As it relates to creating value and assessing what the value of a business
is, the starting point, as Dwayne had mentioned, is revenue, expenses, and ultimately
the bottom line – the net income or operating profit of that business. That’s also
known as the cash flow of that business.
A lot of businesses, especially if they're mom and pop, are trying to avoid taxes, so
they're running as much expense through the business as they can.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 15 --

So, as a potential acquirer of that business you need to go through an analysis of
what's going through this business right now. You likely won't have to have those
expenses once you take it over.
You start to strip those expenses away -- what we would call “add backs.”
Those items that you add back into the profitability of the business, because they're
expenses that you know you'll be able to strip out when you take over.
In a lot of cases, they’re personal expenses that the business owners kind of ran
through the business to avoid tax implications of creating too much profit in the
business.
So the number one mistake I see people making is not going through a very detailed
analysis, starting with the revenue and the gross profit, but also going through all of
their operating expenses and making a determination of why is that expense there,
and does it need to remain there once you take the ownership of the business.
10X Advisor Network: How hard is that process you’re describing?
Jeff Jessen: As it relates to the add backs and going and getting financing for a deal,
it's a simple process that you can create. You take the business’ income statement, or
their statement of operations, whatever you want to call that statement, and you're
going through those expense line items. Then, you basically create another column
where you're subtracting out certain expenses.
The process of getting that information from the person selling the business is
obviously a step you want to take during that due diligence, so that you can have the
detail of what is in those expense items.
But then once you know what's there you just create that column of deductions from
an expense standpoint. Then, you create another pro forma of that same income
statement or statement of operations and you just talk through with the financial
institution.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 15 --

You explain the operating cashflow flow of this business on a go-forward basis, and
that you’re confident that these add backs, these expenses that the current owner is
taking out of the business right now are going to be added back into the business when
you take over.
Most lenders that lend on, especially on acquisitions. And they understand those
concepts.
10X Advisor Network: Dwayne, going back to what you were saying about the
common mistakes a buyer could make… Do you have suggestions for how acquisition
entrepreneurs can handle business acquisitions with large customer concentrations?
Dwayne Baker: I would suggest anybody who has not done this before get advice
from someone who has had experience here. Especially in the due diligence process.
You need to know what to obtain from the seller.
What I would tell you in terms of things to look out for, as part of the purchase
agreement, you can always put covenants in there of things you expect to see. One we
typically see is either a work-out period or an earn-out period. If I'm going to pay
somebody $ 1 million for a business, I don't pay them and they hand over the keys and
walk away on day one. They're typically still in it for 18 to 24 months to help transition
and help ease the pain of you taking over the business.
Additionally, what we're seeing a lot of, too is, sometimes those owners keep some
equity in the business.
You're able to help them take some of it off the table, just so they have a vested
interest that you succeed. I would never think of a situation where you would just buy
something, they hand it over and walk away.
10X Advisor Network: Are there common mistakes in the deal structures you see
from acquisition entrepreneurs?
Dwayne Baker: Yeah, sure. Let’s talk about the operating agreement for the
company. If you buy the entire company, you don't have other investors in the deal,
it’s all on you.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 15 --

If you come in as a partner, however, and especially if you're a minority, you just have
to make sure that there's provisions in there that protect you against something like a
capital call. For example, if the other partners make a capital call on you, and you
don't actually put-up money when the company makes a capital call, then you could
get diluted down.
There are other provisions you can have, too. For example, if partners don’t get alone
there’s something called a shotgun provision where, if the partners are deadlocked
and can't make a decision, one person can just buy the other one out.
There are a lot of things in an operating agreement that you could run into that could
reduce your share of equity or dilute your share of equity in the business or require
you to come up with cash infusion for the business.
Also, anytime you're going to bring other investors on or give someone equity in the
business, you must really understand what tax impacts that could create, or tax
repercussions that could create for whoever's going to be in the business as another
equity holder.
Most of the times when we see new business owners get in this, they've been W-2
employees their entire life, so your tax structure is totally different.
When you become an owner of a business, if you're not a S-corp or a C-corp, you're
going to get a K-1 as a partner in the LLC, and that changes your entire tax structure
and your obligations to make quarterly tax payments, what you can deduct in terms of
benefits, things like that, all things you need to make sure you have a competent tax
advisor and attorney to guide you through so you don't find yourself in a big tax hole
at the end of the year.
10X Advisor Network: Jeff, what are some missed financing opportunities you see
across deals?
Jeff Jessen: I think the most common structure that is left off the table is what we
would call a combination structure with both debt and equity as part of the deal. In
this type of deal, the seller is financing a portion of the ultimate purchase price.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 15 --

So, let's say the purchase price of the deal is $2 million, you're going to pay $500,000
upfront, there's going to be a $500,000 note that the operations of that business have
to pay back before the end of that payment term.
So, the final $1 million might be spread over another year where that's going to be
paid over a 12 month period after the debt's been repaid.
And you structure it so that the business that you're buying can basically be the
foundation of how the acquisition's being paid for, rather than it coming out of your
pocket as the person buying the business.
And most people that have never been through this process, they think they must give
up the cash right up front. They don’t know to make it as small cash investment as
possible, and then make all of the other pieces work so that you can try and finance it
through the business that you're buying, which ultimately you start to take into
account with projections you're making, and you start to look at what is the cash flow
of this business going to be, and what can this business actually take on.
You can structure your deal based around that and say, “I'm going to pay this deal over
36 months based around that. The first 12 are going to be a debt repayment and then the
next 24 months is going to be the rest of the purchase price."
And you as the owner you can either have them stay on for that whole 36-month
period or once the debt’s repaid, maybe they have the ability to be out of the deal.
Being creative with the combination of how you treat cash upfront versus funding it
over time is often missed.
Dwayne Baker: Say there are almost unlimited options. You can be as creative as you
want, especially when you're trying to acquire a small business like we're talking
about here. There are not a lot of shareholders involved.
It's normally between you and one or two, three other people. And if the seller is
burned-out and they're ready to get out of the business, I mean, the sky's the limit in
terms of different variations that you can come up with to take control of the business.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 15 --

10X Advisor Network: Does a lack of understanding on the seller’s side of those
creative options often affect a deal?
Jeff Jessen: Sure, it's something you have to take into account. There's a potential
that they haven't been through a deal either as the seller. And so making sure that
when you're proposing the deal, that you've thought through all of the pieces of it
including what impact your proposal might have on them.
Then, take the time to show them how the deal works. Say, “if we structure it this way,
you get an additional interest payment on that initial debt” or, whatever the case may
be.
In that case, though, you’d explain how they’d be getting an extra certain percentage
above what the purchase price is because of that interest expense and that they’re
going to have a steady income stream over that time.
If it’s still a problem, you could also write into the deal that it's a guaranteed payment,
so that if the business can't afford it, you can promise you’re going to take on that
liability yourself as the acquirer.
So, you put some provisions around it, and you put a strong explanation around the
proposal so that someone that is coming in or someone that's selling their business
and has never done this before, can see all the details.
Clarity is the key. Confusion is what causes failure.
If someone feels like they're confused, then there's a chance the deal's not going to go
through.
So, try to create as much clarity around the terms that you're laying out, why you're
laying them out the way you are and making sure that the seller understands that
you're trying to be fair in the deal, but you're also trying to make sure that both set of
parties are taken care of under the deal.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 15 --

Dwayne Baker: Unless you're going to use just whole numbers, absolute numbers as
to what the payment structure is going to be, say a half million dollars in year one, if
you leave somebody in and you give them a percentage of the upside and they like that
deal, you must be clear on, one, how you're going to calculate that upside. What is it
based on?
If you say gross revenues, define what gross revenues means. So on and so forth. So
there's no ambiguity around what the payment terms are, when you're going to get
paid and how it's going to be calculated and paid out.
10X Advisor Network: Brandon’s taught that the quality of your questions
determines your outcome. What kind of questions do you see people NOT asking that
could tip them off when to walk away from a deal.
Dwayne Baker: The main question a buyer needs to ask a seller is: “Why do you want
to sell this?”
If the seller has a business that they’ve been in for 20 years and it's putting a million
dollars a year in their pocket or half million dollars, whatever that is, why do they
want to walk away from it?
And I don't think people spend enough time on that question.
You can make the math work a hundred different ways and a model's a model until
you put the model into a real-life situation and the model doesn't work out. So, you
need to know why they’re selling.
Second, you need to have clear expectations. What will you have to do as an operator
to make the business run?
Because you may think you have an idea but if the owner is working 80 hours a week
now and they're just tired of it, don't think you can achieve the same success
necessarily doing it 30 hours a week. So, there's that aspect a lot of people miss.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 15 --

Other things would that help me walk away, that I would probably walk away from the
deal on, sometimes you just run into owners that, maybe there's some litigation
against the company. There's just a big risk out there, balloon payments due for some
other liabilities they may have that they don't completely resolve and that you might
not have a lot of confidence in you being able to repay. Things like that.
You need to be careful of litigation that could come back on you after you take
ownership. As a business, making sure that you maybe have an insurance policy or
something in place. But sometimes you just got to weigh the risk of the downside. And
if you spend some time on that instead of just thinking of just the upside, make sure
you're taking some risk into account with it.
Jeff Jessen: Making sure you understand the seller’s role within the organization is
very important. If they are a critical component, we always talk about, a business
becomes the most valuable when the owner isn't needed in the business anymore.
So, if this owner is still a key component, whether it's customer relationships or
they're the main sales, or whatever it is… You need to understand that in the initial
discussion.
Not just why are they getting out of the business? But also, what is their role in the
business?
Because you either have to duplicate that role or you're going to lose. For example, if
it's customer related where they have the relationship, you have that potential of
losing that relationship.
And then the third piece you need to ask about is what is the owner’s relationship to
their employees? Asking about the number of employees they have and if they have
employment agreements in place is important.
You need to know what the structure of the employment foundation is?
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 15 --

Because a lot of times a business gets acquired and then all the employees leave
because they had this relationship with the owner and they don't have employment
agreements in place and they don't have anything that ties them to the business long
term.
In that case, you’ll have to start from scratch, even if you have your customer base of
hiring new employees.
10X Advisor Network: What do you do in the case where there are no employee
agreements in place?
Jeff Jessen: I don't think it's a black and white risk that you just walk away from.
Those employees are typically key employees in the business that would know if the
owner's thinking of selling the business.
So, you would want to understand if the key employees are going to be in the know on
the deal. And if they are, you can make the deal contingent on getting some
employment agreements in place that would say, those key employees are going to
stick with the business for a certain amount of time afterwards.
Ultimately, you can't handcuff the employees to the business. So there is still a chance
they could walk. But if you have an employment agreement, at least it gives you more
confidence as the buyer that those employees are going to stick through that time
period and by then, you should be able to understand exactly their roles and be able to
create some duplication in what they do so that you're not dependent on those roles.
10X Advisor Network: For keyman situations like that, how commonly do you
recommend cutting those key employees in on the deal? Especially if, say the buyer
wants to buy and then pass a lot of the day-to-day operations over to someone?
Jeff Jessen: In a lot of cases, you're not going to be able to negotiate that as part of
the deal. Because creating an equity plan for key employees takes time. There are a lot
of pieces to that, like the W-2 versus all of a sudden, they become a K-1 as a partner in
that business. So, all of those factors take time.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 15 --

So, if you want to get a deal done, the whole negotiation process is going to create
some conflict. But you at least want to identify those people through that due
diligence process. And if you can put some type of employment agreement in place,
great.
Once the deal closes, you want to instantly go to them and talk to them about the big
picture. Brandon, Dwayne, and I always talk about, at some point in your business
growth cycle, that you're going to have to have partners in the business that are key
employees.
You're going to have to align them with what the business is going to do long term, or
else your best people are going to leave because they're looking for something bigger,
something better, something that they have some stake in the game in.
And so, as you continue to plan and grow businesses, you need to just account for the
fact that at some point in time, you're going to have to give equity in up to those key
employees.
So, as you're buying a business, you go in with that same strategy. Day one, I'm going
to paint the vision of where I want this business to go and I'm going to bring in those
key people and say, "I want you to be part of this. When we hit these milestones, this is
your opportunity."
And you just lay it out for them and that should help also keep them aligned with
where you're going.
10X Advisor Network: Are there particular industries where buyers are going to need
to have highly specialized advisors?
Dwayne Baker: Certainly, anything that has to do with medical. Anything where
there's a licensed professional involved, I mean even HVAC and plumbing. I mean, a
plumber is a licensed professional.
So you need to understand that if you are not a licensed plumber, who's going to... who
are you going to... whose license are you planning to operate under? You know? We see
that with some of our small health businesses.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 15 --

Regardless of whether you have a doctor on site, I still need a medical director to
oversee certain functions, and their ways to do that. You can contract with a medical
support office that can do that for you. Different things like that.
I would say additionally, if as part of the acquisition, there is a lot of intellectual
property involved, any trademarks, patents…things that you know are key that you're
paying a premium for, that are key to operating that business, make sure that those
are transferable, and you know when they're going to expire and you know what
rights you're going to have to it. Make sure they come over cleanly with the business.
That they're not tucked into some other entity somewhere.
10X Advisor Network: On the financing side, are there industries where there are
more opportunities to do creative financing like leveraging assets?
Jeff Jessen: Any business that has a strong asset profile would be a stronger business
to acquire as it comes to financing that business because you're able to leverage those
assets, whether they come in the form of receivables that are guaranteed to be paid, or
inventory that the business is holding, or equipment that the business has purchased
that is critical to the operation of that business.
All of those become leverage points when you're working with financial institutions.
What financial institutions look for is cash and other assets that you have available,
and those should be in greater quantities than the liabilities that you have.
So, a bunch of equipment with debt tied to it is not as leverageable as equipment that's
outright owned. Same thing with inventory. If you have inventory but you also have a
line of credit that is tied to that inventory. So, if you have a big inventory balance, the
line of credit is stretched out, then those become not as valuable.
But anything that you have on the asset side that's clean and doesn't have debt
associated with it of any kind becomes an option for leverage.
10X Advisor Network: Are there any interesting ways you recommend for sourcing
potential deals?
Dwayne Baker: I would contact anyone in your network.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 15 --

Ask business owners you know or one your network introduces you to: “What is your
succession plan? How much longer do you want to stay in this business? When do you want
to get out? What does retirement or whatever look like for you?”
Next, is just anybody in your community. People you go to church with, people you
hang out in different social clubs with, just make sure that you're promoting that
you're looking for businesses and what type of business, and then that opens up a lot
of doors.
You’d be surprised how many people say, "Oh, my father in-law or my brother in-law
has actually got an HVAC business and he's looking to sell it, because he's ready to retire."
You'll never know that unless you're promoting that you're looking for a business to
acquire.
10X Advisor Network: How does someone listening to this get your help when
they’re acquiring a business?
Dwayne Baker: We just recently launched 10X Buy Sell. It's a business brokerage, a
transaction advisory business that we have. We help both sellers and buyers. One, if
you're a buyer, we help you identify businesses that are out in the marketplace. We
help you determine what the value is, walk you through the due diligence, all the way
through.
Additionally, if you’re ready to sell a business, obviously our network is full of
entrepreneurial individuals that are either looking to buy their first business or,
looking to expand their current business through growth, through acquisition.
So, we have a large network of buyers. We can help coach you through both those
processes and make sure you have the right tax advisors and legal team on your side,
along with valuation and just helping you go through the entire process.
If you are aware that you're ready to buy a business and especially if you've never had
any operational experience before, let’s still have that conversation.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

We'll determine if you're ready to buy a business and what type of business you think
